claim_text: In the currently coded Ohio matched triads, safety rows more often show formal impasse or interest-arbitration backstop language, while matched non-safety rows are better evidenced through classification, wage-grade, or grievance-administration channels.
claim_scope: Current GABRIEL/codify evidence for Ohio matched cities only; Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati, and Toledo; verified-present rows only for primary support.
counterevidence_summary: Ohio non-safety rows are not mechanism-free; they show wage-grade, classification, and grievance machinery, and not_found interest-arbitration rows cannot prove full-document absence.
reasoning_summary: The same-state, same-city-cycle design makes Ohio the cleanest current triad comparison. Verified safety interest/impasse evidence appears in multiple Ohio safety sources, while verified non-safety evidence tends to sit in classification or grievance administration.
key_limitations: Cincinnati fire and police-supervisor windows have zero verified-present rows; Cleveland fire has a previously flagged/unverified interest row excluded from support; codify windows are not full-document searches.
what_would_change_our_mind: Additional verified-present non-safety interest/impasse rows in Ohio matched cities, or failure to reproduce safety interest/impasse evidence in full-document coding, would weaken this claim.
additional_sources_needed: Full-document or broader-window codify for Ohio non-safety rows; Ohio SERB impasse/arbitration materials for the same cities and cycles; repeat cycles in Columbus/Cleveland/Cincinnati/Toledo.
notes: Bounded to evidence pattern, not wage effect size.

claim_text: Texas evidence is consistent with institutionally uneven safety wage-setting, but the current Texas comparison is not a clean statewide safety-versus-non-safety design outside Houston.
claim_scope: Current Texas codified contracts and report source inventory; Houston, Austin, and San Antonio only.
counterevidence_summary: Houston has a genuine non-safety row and even non-safety impasse/arbitration evidence; Austin uses EMS as safety-adjacent rather than non-safety; San Antonio is police/fire-only.
reasoning_summary: The evidence and source inventory point in the same direction: Texas has strong safety/public-safety statutory channels, but comparison-unit availability is uneven across cities.
key_limitations: Texas non-safety support outside Houston is thin; Austin EMS is not a clean non-safety comparator; San Antonio lacks a non-safety row; source-production effects may drive the pattern.
what_would_change_our_mind: Public, in-window San Antonio or Austin general municipal non-safety CBAs with comparable codified evidence would change the Texas comparison from structurally uneven to potentially triadic.
additional_sources_needed: More Texas non-safety sources if publicly available, especially San Antonio/Austin clerical, public works, or general municipal units; repeat Houston cycles.
notes: This is a source/evidence-structure claim, not a substantive wage-effect claim.

claim_text: Massachusetts is the current corpus’s densest cross-occupation comparison base, supporting bounded safety/non-safety mechanism comparison in Franklin and Seekonk while still falling short of a uniform state grid.
claim_scope: Current Massachusetts codified subset; primarily Franklin and Seekonk, with Boston, Georgetown, Somerville, and Wayland as partial additional context.
counterevidence_summary: Not every Massachusetts city has every occupation class codified; Somerville and Newton safety rows remain unmatched in coverage; some codified MA rows have zero verified-present attributes.
reasoning_summary: Franklin and Seekonk each supply multiple occupation classes in overlapping cycles, allowing mechanism contrasts without jumping immediately to national claims.
key_limitations: Massachusetts remains partly uncodified; city coverage is uneven; current evidence is mechanism presence rather than wage outcomes.
what_would_change_our_mind: Codifying the remaining Massachusetts contracts, or finding that uncodified rows materially reverse the current Franklin/Seekonk pattern, would change this claim.
additional_sources_needed: Codify the remaining Massachusetts rows; add repeat cycles in Franklin/Seekonk; add matched non-safety rows for Somerville/Newton safety units where available.
notes: Useful for report framing but should be presented as breadth-with-limits.

claim_text: The strongest current safety mechanism pattern is not generic occupational hardship alone, but occupational pressure paired with channels that convert pressure into compensation language: overtime, callback, premium pay, minimum staffing, and formal impasse backstops.
claim_scope: Current verified-present evidence across MA/TX/OH safety rows, with non-safety rows used as explicit counterpoints.
counterevidence_summary: Non-safety units also show wage-conversion machinery, and Houston non-safety has impasse/arbitration-type evidence; binary codify cannot rank mechanism intensity.
reasoning_summary: The claim links observed pressure clauses to observed contractual conversion mechanisms rather than treating safety work itself as the measured causal mechanism.
key_limitations: No wage effect sizes; no national sample; codify detects presence not importance; pressure-to-compensation logic is inferred from co-occurrence and clause function.
what_would_change_our_mind: If broader evidence showed similar hazard/minimum-staffing/premium/impasse bundles in non-safety matched units, or safety rows lacked these channels in repeat cycles, this claim would weaken.
additional_sources_needed: Repeat cycles for current cities; additional matched triads in states with public CBAs; full-document coding of minimum-staffing and premium-pay sections.
notes: Central interpretive claim but should stay provisional and mechanism-focused.

claim_text: In the currently coded non-safety evidence, the clearest wage-setting channel is classification, grade, step, or administrative adjustment language rather than safety-specific hazard or minimum-staffing language.
claim_scope: Current verified-present non-safety rows across Massachusetts and Ohio; Texas Houston non-safety treated as a counterpoint.
counterevidence_summary: Houston non-safety shows impasse/arbitration-type language; not all non-safety windows return classification evidence; non-safety wage restraint/admin language is rare in codify output.
reasoning_summary: Multiple non-safety sources contain concrete salary schedules, grade structures, or reclassification terms, making this the cleanest non-safety wage-channel claim currently available.
key_limitations: The attribute may be under-detected because classification schedules can appear in appendices outside curated windows; the claim does not imply non-safety wages are weak or unpressured.
what_would_change_our_mind: Broader non-safety coding showing frequent hazard/minimum-staffing/impasse bundles, or sparse classification evidence in full-document windows, would revise the claim.
additional_sources_needed: Broader non-safety windows with wage appendices; more Texas non-safety units; repeat cycles in Franklin/Seekonk/Wayland/Cleveland.
notes: A good counterpoint claim for a claim-centered report.

claim_text: The current evidence supports a report-ready methodological distinction between interest/formal impasse arbitration and grievance or contract-interpretation arbitration; the San Antonio police contract is the clearest within-document test.
claim_scope: Current codify evidence and audits, especially San Antonio police, Toledo police, Houston fire, and Somerville police/JLMC award.
counterevidence_summary: Ordinary grievance arbitration is common across safety and non-safety CBAs, so reports must not equate any arbitration clause with wage-setting impasse arbitration.
reasoning_summary: The same San Antonio police source contains both a Chapter 174 impasse procedure and a separate grievance-arbitration clause, and codify separated them under different attributes.
key_limitations: This is a coding/method claim, not a substantive wage-gap claim; some windows may omit one arbitration form even when present elsewhere in a full document.
what_would_change_our_mind: If full-document review showed repeated conflation by codify, or if many interest-arbitration positives were actually grievance-only clauses, the distinction would need revision.
additional_sources_needed: Additional impasse/arbitration awards or factfinding documents in Ohio and Massachusetts; reviewer audit of all interest-arbitration positives.
notes: Use this as a guardrail in the next report.

claim_text: Peer/comparator wage evidence should currently be stated only as a narrow and underdeveloped claim: codify verifies one Somerville comparator-style row, while San Antonio shows a documented false negative.
claim_scope: Current evidence layer plus expanded Texas/Ohio audit; not a general absence claim.
counterevidence_summary: Codify missed genuine San Antonio comparator language documented in the audit; the single verified Somerville row is too thin for a broad comparator claim.
reasoning_summary: The claim is mainly about current evidentiary status: verified comparator evidence exists, but the attribute is underpowered and at least once falsely negative.
key_limitations: Only one verified-present row; false negatives known; likely source-type mismatch because comparator reasoning may appear in arbitration/factfinding documents rather than base CBAs.
what_would_change_our_mind: Additional verified comparator rows across multiple cities or a refined codebook re-run that captures San Antonio comparator language would move this from gap claim to substantive mechanism claim.
additional_sources_needed: Interest-arbitration awards, factfinding reports, wage studies, and broader windows around appendices/attachments; codebook refinement/re-run for comparator language.
notes: Do not use not_found comparator rows as evidence of true absence.

claim_text: A substantive Texas non-safety comparison outside Houston is not report-ready: the current evidence has one genuine Houston non-safety source, one Austin safety-adjacent EMS source, and no San Antonio non-safety comparator.
claim_scope: Texas source-inventory and current codified evidence only.
counterevidence_summary: Texas safety evidence is stronger than Texas non-safety evidence; the limitation is source coverage, not a proven absence of non-safety mechanisms.
reasoning_summary: The existing Houston and Austin evidence helps identify the gap, but it cannot support an outside-Houston Texas non-safety claim.
key_limitations: No in-window San Antonio non-safety row; Austin EMS is safety-adjacent; Houston is a single-city base; no new source collection in this run.
what_would_change_our_mind: Public San Antonio or Austin general municipal CBAs, or credible evidence that such units are unavailable for institutional reasons, would change the status.
additional_sources_needed: Targeted public-source verification for Texas non-safety units if available; otherwise document institutional non-availability as a design limitation.
notes: This is intentionally the not-report-ready claim required by the task.
